title: Chapter 3 Master Quiz
description: Test your mastery of operators, logic, and truthiness.

questions.question: Which operator checks if both the value AND the data type are exactly the same?
questions.explanation: Strict equality (===) prevents JavaScript from implicitly converting types before comparison.

questions.question: The double equals (==) operator converts data types so that "10" == 10 evaluates to true.
questions.explanation: Double equals (==) is known as loose equality. It converts different types to a common type before complaining.

questions.question: What is the logical AND operator in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: The double ampersand (&&) represents the logical AND operator.

questions.question: What is the result of using the Logical OR (||) operator: (true || false)?
questions.explanation: The OR operator (||) only requires ONE side to be true for the entire expression to be true.

questions.question: The NOT operator (!) reverses a boolean, so !false becomes true.
questions.explanation: The exclamation mark (!) is the logical NOT operator, which simply flips a boolean to its opposite.

questions.question: Which of the following is considered a 'Falsy' value in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: The number 0 is one of the 6 core falsy values. Options with strings containing any characters (including space) are truthy.

questions.question: Which of the following is considered 'Truthy' in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: All numbers other than 0 (and NaN) are truthy. Null, undefined, and empty string are falsy.

questions.question: Based on operator precedence, what is the result of: 5 + 2 * 3?
questions.explanation: Multiplication (*) happens before addition (+). So 2 * 3 = 6, then 5 + 6 = 11.

questions.question: How do you correctly force addition to happen BEFORE multiplication in an expression?
options: Put addition at the end of the line
questions.explanation: Parentheses ( ) have the highest precedence in JavaScript and always execute first.

questions.question: Which operator checks if a value is NOT equal to another value (strictly)?
questions.explanation: Strict inequality (!==) checks if values or types do not match, without doing type conversion.

questions.question: The operator that asks a question, followed by a ?, and has two possible outcomes separated by a : is called the _____ operator.
questions.explanation: The ternary operator is the only 3-part operator in JavaScript.

questions.question: What is the output of: let win = (5 > 10) ? "Yes" : "No";
questions.explanation: Because 5 is not greater than 10, the condition is false. The ternary operator returns the second value: "No".

questions.question: In JavaScript, the single equals sign (=) is used to check if two things are equal.
questions.explanation: The single equals sign (=) is for ASSIGNMENT, meaning it gives a value to a variable. To check equality, use == or ===.

questions.question: Which of these expressions returns true?
questions.explanation: 10 is not less or greater than 10, but it IS less than OR EQUAL to 10 (<=).

questions.question: What is the output of: console.log(Boolean(null));?
questions.explanation: Because null is one of the 6 falsy values, converting it to a Boolean returns false.

questions.question: If userAge is 20, what is the result of: (userAge >= 18) && (userAge <= 65)?
questions.explanation: Both conditions are true (20 is greater than 18 AND less than 65). AND (&&) requires both to be true, so it returns true.

questions.question: What is the truthiness of an empty object: {} ?
questions.explanation: Empty objects ({}) and arrays ([]) are not on the list of 6 Falsy values. Therefore, they are Truthy.

questions.question: What happens when you chain multiple ternary operators together?
options: It becomes very hard to read
questions.explanation: While legal, chaining ternaries is considered bad practice because it produces highly unreadable 'spaghetti' code.

questions.question: The _____ operator only evaluates to false if BOTH sides of it are false.
questions.explanation: The OR (||) operator is very forgiving; as long as one side is true, the whole thing is true.

questions.question: The string "0" is a Falsy value because it contains a zero.
questions.explanation: A string containing a zero is still a string with text characters in it! It is Truthy.

questions.question: What does an expression inside an IF statement always evaluate down to?
questions.explanation: Any comparison or logical check always boils down to a single Boolean (true or false).

questions.question: What does strict inequality !== check?
options: If either value OR type are different
If both value AND type are the same
questions.explanation: Strict inequality returns true if the items do not match in value, or do not match in type.
